title: Everyday Slang
description: Sixteen casual Turkish expressions young native speakers actually use - the slang that makes you sound natural instead of like a phrasebook.
situation: Hanging out with friends, texting, and everyday casual chat where formal Turkish would sound stiff.

phrases.tip: Squashed-together form of 'ne haber' (literally 'what news'). The default casual greeting among friends. Reply with 'iyilik' (all good) or 'senden naber?' (what's up with you).
Short for 'kan kardeşi' (blood brother). The go-to friendly address term for a close friend, used by everyone. 'Kanki' is an even softer, cuter version.
Warm, enthusiastic 'fantastic'. Widely acceptable in any setting, from texting a friend to replying to your boss.
Borrowed from European 'super' and fully Turkish now, spelled with the umlaut u: süper. Drop it as a one-word reply meaning 'perfect, sounds good'.
From Arabic 'wallahi' (by God), but used totally casually now just to add emphasis: 'valla bilmiyorum' = 'honestly, I don't know'. Sprinkled everywhere in speech.
Literally 'give it empty'. A breezy way to wave something off. 'Boş ver ya' softens it even more, like 'ah, don't worry about it'.
The all-purpose sigh of mild frustration or weariness. 'Of' is the groan, 'ya' is the filler tail. Said when something is annoying or tiring.
The single most common Turkish filler word. Tacked onto the end of sentences for emphasis or softening: 'gel ya' = 'oh come on, come'. Means nothing on its own, it is just vibe.
'Takılıyoruz' = 'we're just hanging out'. The casual verb for spending time with friends doing nothing in particular. Literally 'to get caught / linger'.
Literally 'no more'. The classic reaction of disbelief when you hear something surprising or unbelievable. Said with wide eyes.
From 'çılgın' (crazy). Used like 'that's insane' for something impressive or shocking. 'Çılgın gibi' = 'like crazy', e.g. studying or working a lot.
Literally 'I ate my head'. Said when you're stressed, overwhelmed, or something is driving you nuts. Playful and very common.
Literally 'my money doesn't exist'. The everyday way to say you have no cash. 'Beş parasızım' (I'm penniless) is a stronger, more dramatic version.
Literally 'I got very tired'. The standard way to say you're wiped out. 'Bittim' (I'm finished) is the more dramatic, slangy version.

phrases.english: let's go / come on, let's go
phrases.tip: 'Hadi' is the all-purpose 'come on / let's' rallying word. 'Hadi bakalım' = 'alright, off we go'. Used constantly to get moving or kick something off.
Literally 'legend / myth'. Used by younger speakers for something epic: 'konser efsaneydi' = 'the concert was legendary'. The Turkish equivalent of calling something 'iconic'.
